name: Aeneas — LLBC to Pure Functional Programs
summary: Son Ho & Protzenko's Aeneas translates a borrow-checked Rust subset (LLBC) into a pure lambda-calculus, eliminating heap reasoning so users prove properties in F* / Coq / Lean / HOL4 over functional programs. Backward functions encode mutable-borrow updates as pure function calls, sidestepping prophecies. Borrow-checker proven sound via symbolic execution (ICFP 2024 follow-up).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Son Ho & Protzenko's **Aeneas** translates a borrow-checked Rust subset (LLBC, Low-Level Borrow Calculus) into a **pure lambda-calculus**, eliminating heap reasoning so users prove properties in F\* / Coq / Lean / HOL4 over functional programs.

**LLBC + symbolic borrow semantics** gives a pure functional semantics to safe Rust, including mutable borrows. **Backward functions**: a novel encoding that turns a mutable borrow's "give back the updated value" into a pure function call, sidestepping prophecies. Borrow-checker proven sound via symbolic execution (ICFP 2024 follow-up). Active research toolchain; used by Microsoft (Protzenko) for crypto-adjacent verification; safe-Rust only today, with separation-logic extensions for unsafe in progress.

This is the third major axis among Rust verifiers — alongside Prusti's separation-logic-via-Viper approach and Creusot's prophecy-via-WhyML approach, Aeneas eliminates heap entirely and produces pure functional code that any standard proof assistant can reason about.

sections.title: Backward functions and pure-functional translation
sections.content:
Aeneas's defining trick is the **backward function** encoding. Where Creusot uses prophetic variables `^x` to denote the future value of a borrow, Aeneas synthesises a separate **backward function** for each mutable borrow: a pure function that, given the borrowed value and the updated state, returns the post-borrow state of the original. A Rust function `fn f(x: &mut T) -> R` becomes a pair of functions in the target language — one returning `R` (the forward function), one returning the updated `T` (the backward function).

The net effect is that **all heap reasoning is compiled away** before the proof obligations reach the user. Specifications and proofs operate on pure values and pure functions, with no separation-logic permissions or prophecy variables. Standard induction principles work; ordinary functional-correctness proofs apply. The cost is the source language restriction: Aeneas requires that LLBC programs satisfy borrow-checker constraints stricter than full Rust, and the symbolic-execution-based soundness proof (ICFP 2024) defines what subset is admissible.

sections.title: Target backends and proof assistant choice
sections.content:
Aeneas extracts to multiple proof assistants — F\*, Coq, Lean 4, and HOL4 — letting the user pick whichever ecosystem suits the verification target. This is unusual among Rust verifiers; Prusti is tied to Viper/Z3, Creusot to Why3, Verus to Z3. The pure-functional output is generic enough that any proof assistant can consume it.

The Microsoft-internal use case (Protzenko, who also works on F\* / Low\* / KaRaMeL — see `karamel-low-star.json`) targets cryptographic primitives where Aeneas extracts Rust source to F\* and the cryptographic correctness proof is then discharged in F\*'s refinement-typed environment.

sections.title: Status and unsafe-Rust extensions
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): active research toolchain, open-source under AeneasVerif/aeneas. Used by Microsoft (Protzenko) for crypto-adjacent Rust verification. Safe-Rust only today; **separation-logic extensions for unsafe Rust** are in progress, which would close the gap with RefinedRust and RustBelt for verifying unsafe libraries.

The ICFP 2024 follow-up paper on symbolic-execution-based borrow-checker soundness puts Aeneas's translation on a foundational footing: not only does the tool work in practice, but the LLBC semantics is proven to faithfully model the Rust subset it consumes.
